speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three. Interview with Johtje Vos. August 13, 1996. What kind of people were hidden? Whom did the group send?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very mixed. We had an artist, we had a doctor, and we had people who had business. It was a very mixed group, but they were all friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people find out about the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as friends, we asked them. We didn't start the group, so I don't remember exactly. Yeah, I think Jan Bartelsman started the group. And so he asked us to become-- to help. He didn't say, do you want to be a member? Because you were not a member, because you just worked with the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did the Jews know to go to the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we talked to the Jews about the group, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of them were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the ones who were hidden with us, for instance, knew all about the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of these people before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, some. Those two famous musicians, I [INAUDIBLE] they were friends before the war already, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very large. We had entry-- three bedrooms and a big dining room and a living room. And in the garden, we had a studio which had two little bedrooms with it. Because when I was divorced and my husband left me, I rented a house to have an income, and I lived in the studio, and in those two little bedrooms that were there. They were actually not even rooms. They were just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your own family stay then? What part of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My old family lived in a very good house, very big house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no. My house, I had rented it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your husband and your children. Where were they living?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were still married? In the main house. In a very nice big house.

speaker: interviewer
text: I meant. you and Mr. Vos and your two daughters. The sons came later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had his-- no, his son was no longer alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to think when I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live during-- when they started sending people to hide, where did you live? Where did you eat? Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the main house, in Leiden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And they? Where did the Jews that were hidden sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere in that-- later we went in ourselves. No, no, no. That's not true. Before that, we went in ourselves. I had rented it with them, and I got too many people to hide. The people who rented it went out, and I lived here myself and could have-- my husband and I could have many more people hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many rooms were available to you, and how many to the people who were hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the living room and the dining room were available for anybody. They were there all day. And we had our own bedroom. When we became more crowded, we took the children in our bedroom with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were these people--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a big attic, and that you-- there was a ladder in the attic which you could let down. Oh yeah, indeed. And that was always down, yeah, that ladder. Except when the Jews went in it, and it could be a lot of Jews in that attic. It was a very large attic. And then they put the ladder up and closed it, so you could hardly see that there wasn't a place there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ventilation there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was a window in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the day, or during normal-- a typical day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did these people do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they visible in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes, yeah, they came all down. We didn't keep them in lock.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I didn't mean that you kept them in lock. What I meant is, was it dangerous for them to be wandering around the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not on regular days. But as soon as there was a Nazi raid, then of course they have to go and disappear. But usually then they left the house through the tunnel and escaped that way. Because we almost always knew when there was a-- that we had a raid totally unexpectedly, that has happened, oh, maybe one or two times. But we always got this two-ring telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everyone disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody would go immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these people come with baggage-- with things, their possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, little. Some clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would it have been obvious to anybody coming to the house that there were other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It would have been obvious, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way? If they were not around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gave us a suitcase, and they looked very Jewish. Then it was obvious.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but if they were not around. You know, a typical day, somebody rang your bell at 3 o'clock in the afternoon, you were not expecting a raid. Would it be obvious that there were people being hidden in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. But that never happened. We always got a warning on the telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about neighbors? Did they know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was rather-- it was a dead end road with a few villas. And so they were not too close. We had a garden surrounding all our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had gardens?

speaker: interviewee
text: A garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And your nearest neighbor, did they ever see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I asked before about your children. And they were in your house, these people, for close to three-- well, more than, about four years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children were growing up. Who did they think they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we never talked about it. They were just friends living with us. And we never discussed-- our oldest son, he knew all about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your husband's son? He knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there ever any chance of him telling anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But he knew. And he sometimes helped us even. One time, there was a-- where was it again where they had to bring something away? I don't remember exactly who it was. But anyway, he knew about it. And we-- oh, yeah, we had false red papers. And when we saw the Germans coming, when we saw that car, they always came in-- any car was always German, because we were not allowed to have cars anymore. And we saw that car stopping front of town. Then they had to come all the way there yet. And so I had-- we had just been sitting at the table with the Jewish people, with their passports, or their identification papers, which I had. And because there was so much danger in the village the previous days, they had decided to go away for a few days to their alternate addresses. And so I sat at the table with those peoples, and then I saw the car stopping.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the Jewish people went at the back of the house and went away from there. But I was sitting there with all those papers. So I took them, and I put them in my son's shirt. And I said to him, go away, very greatly. He knew all about it. He was nine years old only. And he took a ball, and he went out of the front door and said very politely, how do you do? To the people who came, those damn Germans. And he took a ball, and he against the wall. He ball-- with his ball. And he went a little further, and a little further. Than I saw him disappear. And oh, was I thankful that he made it! Which son was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Peter, our oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they-- the Jews went away for a few days, where did they go, and who took them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had addresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other safe havens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had addresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who took them? How did they get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, nothing was safe in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far were the other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some went with bicycles and some walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feed these people? How did they eat? Where did they sleep? Who took care of laundry? How was the daily house run?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did the work with all the other women together.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish women?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish women, yeah. And well, we had 32 Jewish people hidden during the war, but not all at the same time. The most we had at the same time was 14, but that was only a very short period. And so we did the work together, the housework and household and all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get your food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very different sources. We got coupons. And Because I was German, and my two daughters were German, I got double the coupons. So that instead of three, I got six. And that helped already, a lot. And then we got some coupons from the organization, from the underground organization. And so we divided everything. We all were very starved during the war, because it was-- the last winter. That's still known in Holland as 'the hunger winter.' And the hunger winter was terrible. We all were-- and it was terrible because people who really were going to starve from hunger first got very thin, and then they blew up. And that was the last stage. So when we saw somebody blowing up, we all sacrificed a little. Because, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a typical meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no typical meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was soup kitchens, and we all got coupons. Of course, the Jewish people did not get coupons, but I got double coupons, for my two daughters and me. And so we had to go every day to a certain place where there was usually kind of a soup. And when you had the potato from the soup, you celebrated. Because it was practically water. And then there was always one or two potatoes in, still unpeeled and dirt on them. Because it was not exactly a luxury soup. But when you had the potato, oh boy, there was a feast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran those soup kitchens? The Dutch or the German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Appointed people. Appointed people. It was-- it was done, I think, by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any stores or places to buy things?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No open stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those coupons, those rations, were given to the soup kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no, and we had-- yeah. I know [INAUDIBLE] was a store that was always open, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what could you buy there with those coupons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes a little margarine. Sometimes half a bottle of milk. Sometimes some bread. Different-- what they got, they sold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cook at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, if we had something to cook, we cooked it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in the summer? Could you grow vegetables?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we want-- we went to pick mushrooms and every kind of thing which was possibly edible from the fields, of course. But you learn to live with less and less food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did everyone pitch in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. We shared everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get along? You said there were times when people got ornery.

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Amongst themselves, with 14 people plus you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sometimes there were terrible quarrels.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. About anything that you quarrel about, women.

speaker: interviewer
text: The women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was mainly the women who quarreled. You know, everybody was tense. For five years, we were just all the way like this. No, not the entire five years, but certainly for four years, you were tense and afraid, actually, the whole day. You tried not to think about it all the time, but you know. And then there were also-- I was not so worried of the Germans, because I was-- well, I was young and strong, and I was a naughty girl. Had been always, in my life. So I had this-- I'll get those damn Germans, you know? I'm answer them. But I was incredibly afraid of the airplanes that flew over our house. Because they were either English planes with bombs to go to Germany, or German with bombs going to England. And that route was just over our place. And I was scared stiff of that. And still, when I'm now at home and in the night, and airplane passes, I wake up a movement, like this. I never get rid of that anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the disagreements and quarrels-- did people hit each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. There were quarrels. You know, everybody was tense. And that was one of the great things, that my husband had a way of always calming people down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he the mediator? The one who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh sure, yeah. Very much so. When two women would quarrel, he would go there and sit with him and talk to them. He was fantastic in that field. And it was very necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any romances? Anybody get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody give birth there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we got a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So someone came in--

speaker: interviewee
text: From this woman who was the wife of the musician, of Nap De Klyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the second child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- yeah, that was the second child, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped with the birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she go to the hospital or was she there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Oh, no. There was no chance to go to the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped her? Who delivered the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: A doctor friend came and helped her.

speaker: interviewer
text: A doctor friend who was part of the group? Or somebody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if he was a member of the group, but I trusted him fully.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he was caught, later. And then there came a new doctor. And nobody knew whether we could trust him or not. He acted very trustworthy, but you never knew, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if somebody got sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was more difficult when somebody died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do when a Jew dies in your house?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we had-- not in our house, but a house of friends, there was a very nice Jewish man in there. He was a professor, and he was a very, very great person. And we all loved him, and he died. One morning, they found him dead in the bed. He had a heart attack. And we didn't know what to do. But he lived in Amsterdam, and he lived very near a park. So they waited till it was dark, and they dressed him up in a coat. And two men walked with him to that park with him dead in between them. You know, they held him up. And they put him on a bench. What do you call that, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: A bench.

speaker: interviewee
text: A bench. And they even put some little flowers they found near there. And they put that in his hands, and they left him there. And next morning, he was gone. So the Germans probably found him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody have to go-- require hospitalization or emergency care of any sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in our house. And emergency care? There was no emergency. You couldn't bring them to a hospital. Because you couldn't trust the people in the hospital. A hospital would take a Jew, but it was at his own risk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone get married or live together? I don't imagine you had clergy there. Or did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the people we knew, they were all married. They're all young married people. We also had single people, but I don't think there were many love stories. You had all those things you had and loved at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our own children?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, Jewish children. Did you have Jewish-- I know you had the little boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I had Moana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Moana.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was Moana?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moana was the daughter of friends of mine, where we went before it became terribly dangerous for the Jewish people. They lived in Amsterdam. And we told them, it's going to be more dangerous, and come to us. And they didn't want to. He had his principle. He said, this comes over our people, and we have to accept it. Which was, we said, bullshit. You know? We were so mad that they didn't want our help. But in the very last moment, when the Nazi has started the raid on that street, where they lived and many other Jewish people lived, there was a German-- a Dutch electrician who always was-- he had to work for the Germans, that was not his choice-- who went ahead of them. When they were in this house, he was already in one or two houses further, to switch off all the electricity of the houses were the Jews were taken, because they were always afraid of fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came to our house, and there lived this friend of ours with his wife and his little daughter. And he took a chance. He also took a chance on our life, but we didn't know that, of course. And he said, could you bring our daughter to Leiden, to those family that she will not be caught? Because they couldn't escape any more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took it. And that was a terrible risk he took, only your own life. Because they didn't know whether the man was pro-German or not, pro-Hitler. But thank God, he was good. And he took the child in the back of his bicycle, and he bicycled, which was about 27, 28 kilometers, to Leiden and put-- rang the bell, put the girl there. And I knew the girl, of course. And then he said, here she is. Her parents are taken. And he ran away, out on the bicycle. And we never know who he was. We've never been able to find out who did that, which was very heroic, what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moana. And what's her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hillfman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hillfman. And she stayed with you throughout the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she stayed with us forever, almost. She's still my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when she lived in that cooped up where they brought all the Jewish people, she lived with 21 members of her family, and not one survived. So when the war was over, then we kept her. And we wanted to adopt her, but the Jewish people didn't allow it. They said, she has to grow up in a Jewish surrounding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which Jewish people did not allow it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there wasn't a Jewish-- how do you call that-- orphanage, very near us, and that's where she was placed.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. Yeah. In the war she was with us, the whole war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when children cried or screamed when they were supposed to be quiet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when there were Germans coming in, and there were children? How did you keep the children quiet? Little children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our own children? Our own children? They never cried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not your own, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But the older ones, they knew where to go. They knew it exactly. When we said, go, then they knew it's coming. And once, one of the Jewish children stayed, had not gone away, and she was with our children, and the Germans didn't pay any attention to her. Although she was dark, and my two children were highly blonde. As blonde as you can be. But it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever interrogated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Several times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they had a feeling. They always-- they came to our house because they knew there is something going on there. But they didn't know-- but we also had an artist, a Jewish artist, who falsified papers. He could beautifully do that. Beautiful work he did. And so that office we had there too. Kurt was his name, Kurt Delmonte.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kurt, K-U-R-T, Delmonte.

speaker: interviewer
text: Delmonte. One word?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was an artist?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an artist. I have several of his works yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were talking about being interrogated by the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, they came and knew something is going on in this house, because they came time and time again. But I never broke down. I kept saying, nonsense. I wouldn't do a thing like that. You know? And I am German, so I wouldn't do it-- again, 'my own people.' You know? I faked all that, of course. But I was German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Could you describe--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they asked me, where is your husband? And I didn't say that I had a second husband. I said, my husband is away in Russia, fighting. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he was German. They did not know you had remarried?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And I kept my name. Not officially, but I introduced myself to them. My first husband's name was Molenaar, which was a Dutch name. His forefathers were Dutch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Could you describe an interrogation? Where was it held, and what was said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was in my own house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever take you out of your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to interrogate--

speaker: interviewee
text: They threatened very often to take me out, but they never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to interrogate?

speaker: interviewee
text: German officers, one or two. And they started throwing questions, throwing questions, throwing questions till I was so tired that they hoped that I would make a mistake and slip, which I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your greatest fear that you would do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My fear was that they would come in a moment that they all were around. That we had no warning. But we always had a warning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Were you ever jailed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Were I ever--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever jailed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put in jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My husband was. My husband, he once took my husband, and he was for three days in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they take him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they suspected him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he didn't know that-- they didn't know he was your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Sure they know. Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, they did know.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him from my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point they knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh sure, they knew, yeah. We married in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I know. When there was a real emergency, how did they get into the tunnel? And what was the tunnel?

speaker: interviewee
text: The tunnel was in the atelier, in the back of the house where most of them lived, because it had two bedrooms with it and a living room. They had built it all out. Because when I was divorced from my first husband, I lived here. So we added some to it. And the tunnel went from that place. The tunnel was not from the house. It was from that place. So the Jewish people who were in the house would run from the back door immediately into there, in there. And then there was a-- there was an opening with a wall which you could put aside. You couldn't see that at all. We had to try that out with all kinds of lights, and even with a glass pot and the sun shining in it. So we knew they could never find that opening if they didn't know. And there, they could slide that away, and he could get in the coal bin. And from the coal bin he could get in the [INAUDIBLE].

words.text: And from the coal bin he could

speaker: interviewer
text: What covered the glass? How did you camouflage it? With what?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not camouflaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, outside? Oh, yeah. I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you'll show me in the picture, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a coal bin. And we made a double spot in that coal bin so that up were some coals. And under that, it was empty. And from there, they went in that tunnel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could they close off the tunnel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Besides, I always let them in and closed it off myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And when they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, we had-- we had a non-Jewish hider who was sold by the Germans. He was the director of the Royal Blast Furnaces in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- he was sold by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Director.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was not Jewish, but he had to hide because he had-- they had in the blast furnaces some very important stuff. I don't know what the name was, [INAUDIBLE] or something, which the Germans wanted. And he had hidden it himself, personally, the director, and hidden a letter to the arts commandant from the German commander in Holland. And had told him, I, Director-- Van Delden was his name-- have hidden that stuff myself. You'll never get it. If you get me, you'll get it. And then we hid him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Van and then capital D-E-L-D-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Van Delden. And he was a coal-- what was his position?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was director of Royal Blast Furnace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Royal Blast Furnace. So you hid him in the tunnel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And his son lives with us here in Woodstock.

speaker: interviewer
text: His son is alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When people went down into the tunnel, could they stand?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they crawled.

speaker: interviewer
text: They crawled.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They could sit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it took everybody in, all 14?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- were there provisions in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But there were lines, flashlights.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if they had to stay for any length of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: No facility. Was there a radio there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing. Just flashlights.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it was good that there was no radio, because they would have heard it. It was not that deep. And one day, this director from the blast furnaces-- he was such a strange guy. He was a very nice man. And he was not Jewish, but they sought him. And so when he got-- went in that tunnel, he stayed in the entrance instead of going deeper in. And, of course, I didn't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the Germans came, and I always was standing near that entrance. I prefer to stand on it, so that I somehow, you know? And then I heard-- certainly heard [WHISTLING]. He was whistling. Oh, God. And the Germans were standing there with me. So I did-- I suddenly stood up where I was standing-- You can't go away! You can't go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I can't go away. I did so-- [CLAPS] And I said, I'm so cold! I'm so cold! here! And it was not cold at all. But he fell for it. And he heard me trampling, and so he stopped whistling. He suddenly realized what he was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a certain--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was one of the narrow escapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Narrow escapes, right. The group that were with you, the Jews, were there Jewish-- we have to change our tape, I'm sorry.
